title: Mosaic plots
description: Determine which mosaic plot represents a given two-way table and what we can conclude from a given mosaic plot.

assessment_items.question_content:
Tate works at an ice cream stand. Every customer can choose one of two toppings—sprinkles or chocolate chips. He recorded the age group and the chosen topping for a random sample of $120$ customers. Here are the results:

Age group | Sprinkles | Chocolate chips | Neither | Total
- | -: | -: | -: | -:
Children| $27$ | $38$ | $7$ | $72$
Adults | $13$ | $22$ | $13$ | $48$
Total | $40$ | $60$ | $20$ | $120$

**Which mosaic plot correctly displays these results?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Thinking about a mosaic plot

A mosaic plot is like a segmented bar graph where we make the width of each column proportional to the number of individuals in that category.

That makes the area of each segment proportional to the number of individuals in the corresponding cell of two-way table.

Let's look at each mosaic plot to see which one has proportions that relate to the two-way table.

###Mosaic plot A

There are more children than adults, so the column for children should be wider than the column for adults. However, plot A shows more adults than children, so it can't be the right answer.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/b934740e35cda05ab6eaf07f3120887a966a9d4e)

###Mosaic plot B

There are more children than adults, so the column for children should be wider than the column for adults. However, plot B shows about the same number of adults and children, so it can't be the right answer.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/c8519def61143321d88a3aae91ab30dfd8258d7c)

###Mosaic plot C

To correctly create a mosaic plot, first we split the horizontal axis proportionally based on the percentage that each category forms of the total sample. For example, the "Children" column should be $60\%$ of the width of the plot because $72$ out of $120$ customers were children.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/0fb02557f2cc04954632bf6c0114ee2cde4162fe)

Then we split each column proportionally based on the percentage that each vertical category forms of the column total.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/8ff51038e875261f981407914c2889ce90ea2ca5)

(Sometimes, the column totals don't add up to $100\%$ because of rounding — this is nothing to be concerned about.)

So plot C correctly displays the results.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/519d67787d3f1c01ef2389c12ccbcbf83380d48d)

assessment_items.question_content:
Mr. Quintana surveyed students about whether they agreed or disagreed with the statement, "I like school lunch." He recorded the income level of the student's family and the response for a random sample of $330$ students. Here are the results:

 | Agree | Disagree | Total
- | -: | -: | -:
Low income| $42$ | $63$ | $105$
Middle income | $73$ | $65$ | $138$
High income| $55$ | $32$ | $87$
Total| $177$ | $153$ | $330$

**Which mosaic plot correctly displays these results?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Thinking about a mosaic plot

A mosaic plot is like a segmented bar graph where we make the width of each column proportional to the number of individuals in that category.

That makes the area of each segment proportional to the number of individuals in the corresponding cell of two-way table.

Let's look at each mosaic plot to see which one has proportions that relate to the two-way table.

###Mosaic plot B

Among students from low income families, more disagreed about the liking school lunch than agreed. So the segment for disagree should be taller than the segment for agree in the "low" column. However, plot B shows more agreement than disagreement, so it can't be the right answer.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/6a3abef2363e08833768bf30fde470884500b3ff)

###Mosaic plot C

Among students from high income families, more agreed about the liking school lunch than disagreed. So the segment for agree should be taller than the segment for disagree in the "high" column. However, plot C shows more disagreement than agreement, so it can't be the right answer.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/31893cfad35787def3e464291a7501b044e5c27c)

###Mosaic plot A

To correctly create a mosaic plot, first we split the horizontal axis proportionally based on the percentage that each category forms of the total sample. For example, the "High" column should be $26\%$ of the width of the plot because $87$ out of $330$ students were from high income families.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/7b9f30fd6c7635a0228a31935ac43ad10b191533)

Then we split each column proportionally based on the percentage that each vertical category forms of the column total.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/1c3d2e3dd9a23900531f0cb85ed5c10935a9f671)

So plot A correctly displays the results.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/aa04571bcbadc82dc488e23ce625e0c9f9a7aba6)

assessment_items.question_content:
Dr. Harris is curious whether her patients' body images matched their relative sizes, as measured by body mass index. She calculated the body mass index for and surveyed a random sample of $120$ of her patients. Here are the results:

 | Overweight | Not overweight | Total
- | -: | -: | -:
Think they are overweight| $74$ | $13$ | $87$
Think they are not overweight| $10$ | $23$ | $33$
Total| $84$ | $36$ | $120$

**Which mosaic plot correctly displays these results?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Thinking about a mosaic plot

A mosaic plot is like a segmented bar graph where we make the width of each column proportional to the number of individuals in that category.

That makes the area of each segment proportional to the number of individuals in the corresponding cell of two-way table.

Let's look at each mosaic plot to see which one has proportions that relate to the two-way table.

###Mosaic plot A

There are more overweight patients than not overweight patients, so the column for "Overweight - yes" should be wider than the column for "Overweight - no". However, plot A shows about the same number of patients who are overweight or not, so it can't be the right answer.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/f73064b95d597b539520b2738b6cab2c041d66c8)

###Mosaic plot C

There are more overweight patients than not overweight patients, so the column for "Overweight - yes" should be wider than the column for "Overweight - no". However, plot C shows more patients who are not overweight than patients who are overweight, so it can't be the right answer.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/e5b4586ff731f777e3e1ad54ca48c09a20dd0dfd)

###Mosaic plot B

To correctly create a mosaic plot, first we split the horizontal axis proportionally based on the percentage that each category forms of the total sample. For example, the "Overweight - yes" column should be $70\%$ of the width of the plot because $84$ out of $120$ patients were overweight.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/01514ae707c42ef537b4934f4cc7c62ca661139b)

Then we split each column proportionally based on the percentage that each vertical category forms of the column total.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/33b5d00580c07e3d7fbbe028542d601c07da436b)

So plot B correctly displays the results.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/a8e5de0100cae450e6e583aa15d5d0d9535c1fa5)

assessment_items.question_content:
The following table summarizes the locations and number of beds available in the hospitals in Montana in 2019.

 | Rural | Urban | Total
- | -: | -: | -:
$>50$ beds| $17$ | $34$ | $51$
$\leq 50$ beds| $83$ | $8$ | $91$
Total| $100$ | $42$ | $142$

**Which mosaic plot correctly displays these results?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Thinking about a mosaic plot

A mosaic plot is like a segmented bar graph where we make the width of each column proportional to the number of individuals in that category.

That makes the area of each segment proportional to the number of individuals in the corresponding cell of two-way table.

Let's look at each mosaic plot to see which one has proportions that relate to the two-way table.

###Mosaic plot A

There are fewer rural hospitals with more than $50$ beds. So the segment for "$>50$" should be shorter than the segment for "$\leq 50$" in the "Rural" column. However, plot A shows more rural hospitals with greater than $50$ beds, so it can't be the right answer.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/39d25fb207a7807890f3f365c412ebdf486e85ac)

###Mosaic plot C

There are more urban hospitals with greater than $50$ beds than with fewer beds. So the segment for "$>50$" should be taller than the segment for "$\leq 50$" in the "Urban" column. However, plot C shows more urban hospitals with fewer beds, so it can't be the right answer.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/eb6a6d7b2964a5c6fffb852ddacd9a6e88950a3a)

###Mosaic plot B

To correctly create a mosaic plot, first we split the horizontal axis proportionally based on the percentage that each category forms of the total sample. For example, the "Rural" column should be $70\%$ of the width of the plot because $100$ out of $142$ hospitals were in rural locations.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/1a94eb18faad54047102190ce0eb908379f80773)

Then we split each column proportionally based on the percentage that each vertical category forms of the column total.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/915c3170198fee51cce055b075aabb5681bc8f75)

So plot B correctly displays the results.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/15e68d4351ab7fa87414d86fac94c24e2ccb566c)

assessment_items.question_content:
Noelle is a doctor who is screening patients for antibodies for a certain disease. They wonder if the disease is impacting people differently based on geographic region. The following mosaic plot summarizes the test results for Noelle's patients.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/9d06a28aa14f9ae37f34b29e1e9df771845019f2)

**Which of these statements can we justify from the mosaic plot?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Thinking about a mosaic plot

A mosaic plot is like a segmented bar graph where we make the width of each column proportional to the number of individuals in that category.

That makes the area of each segment proportional to the number of individuals in the corresponding cell of two-way table.

Let's see if each statement is one that we can justify from the given mosaic plot.

###"Patients from each region were about equally likely to have antibodies for the disease."

The height of the "Has antibodies" segment is about the same in both columns. So we *can* justify this statement from the mosaic plot.

###"Noelle screened the same number of patients from each region."

The "region 2" column is wider than the "region 1" column. So a larger percentage of the patients Noelle screened were from region 2 than from region 1.

So we *cannot* justify this statement from the mosaic plot.

###"The majority of patients in region 2 have no antibodies to the disease."

The "No antibodies" segment takes up more than half of the height of the "region 2" column. So we *can* justify this statement from the mosaic plot.

###Answers

We can justify these statements from the mosaic plot:

* Patients from each region were about equally likely to have antibodies for the disease.
* The majority of patients in region 2 have no antibodies to the disease.

choices.content: Patients from each region were about equally likely to have antibodies for the disease.
Noelle screened the same number of patients from each region.
The majority of patients in region 2 have no antibodies to the disease.

assessment_items.question_content:
Zach is a human resources specialist. He wonders whether his company promotes employees differently based on their family demographics. The following mosaic plot summarizes the promotions in the past year for employees at Zach's company.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/2a9818c6a7d5f5628a5ed07343b8444aaedc975b)

**Which of these statements can we justify from the mosaic plot?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Thinking about a mosaic plot

A mosaic plot is like a segmented bar graph where we make the width of each column proportional to the number of individuals in that category.

That makes the area of each segment proportional to the number of individuals in the corresponding cell of two-way table.

Let's see if each statement is one that we can justify from the given mosaic plot.

###"Employees with children and no partner were least likely to get promoted."

The height of the "Yes" segment is shorter in the "Children and no partner" column than in the other columns. So we *can* justify this statement from the mosaic plot.

###"Less than $20\%$ of employees were promoted."

The "Yes" segments make up less than $20\%$ of the total area (each with a relative frequency of less than $20\%$ of its respective column) of the mosaic plot.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

So we *can* justify this statement from the mosaic plot.

###"About half of the employees have children."

The combined widths of the "Children and no partner" and "Children and partner" columns are about half the width of the plot, meaning they represent about half of the employees. So we *can* justify this statement from the mosaic plot.

###Answers

We can justify these statements from the mosaic plot:

* Employees with children and no partner were least likely to get promoted.
* Less than $20\%$ of employees were promoted.
* About half of the employees have children.

choices.content: Employees with children and no partner were least likely to get promoted.
Less than $20\%$ of employees were promoted.
About half of the employees have children.

assessment_items.question_content:
A guidance counselor was curious about the relationship between their students' transportation to school and whether they were working a job while in school. The following plot summarizes those characteristics for the school's $480$ students.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/78b18bfb2049533bf60096fe41ecb31117c944be)

**Which of these statements can we justify from the mosaic plot?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Thinking about a mosaic plot

A mosaic plot is like a segmented bar graph where we make the width of each column proportional to the number of individuals in that category.

That makes the area of each segment proportional to the number of individuals in the corresponding cell of two-way table.

Let's see if each statement is one that we can justify from the given mosaic plot.

###"The majority of the students have a job."

The "No" column is wider than the "Yes" column. So a larger percentage of the students did not have a job than had a job.

So we *cannot* justify this statement from the mosaic plot.

###"The fewest students have a job and walk to school."

The area of the "Walking" segment of the "Yes" column is less than the area of any other segment in the plot, meaning that fewer students fit that pair of characteristics than any other pair.

So we *can* justify this statement from the mosaic plot.

###"Students with a job are more likely than students without a job to use private transportation."

If the student has a job, the relative frequency of using private transportation is more than $80\%$, and if they don't have a job, the relative frequency of using private transportation is about $70\%$.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

If we didn't have relative frequencies labeled, we could still see that the "Private" segment was taller in the "Yes" column than in the "No" column.

So we *can* justify this statement from the mosaic plot.

###Answers

We can justify these statements from the mosaic plot:

* The fewest students have a job and walk to school.
* Students with a job are more likely than students without a job to use private transportation.

choices.content: The majority of the students have a job.
The fewest students have a job and walk to school.
Students with a job are more likely than students without a job to use private transportation.

assessment_items.question_content:
A legislator wants information to guide their decision about whether to create a law requiring motorcycle cyclists to wear a helmet while riding. The following plot summarizes the outcomes of the motorcycle accidents in their region in the past year.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/5f8a09014f170e9453fe4c9c82bbbcb2347a3788)

**Which of these statements can we justify from the mosaic plot?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Thinking about a mosaic plot

A mosaic plot is like a segmented bar graph where we make the width of each column proportional to the number of individuals in that category.

That makes the area of each segment proportional to the number of individuals in the corresponding cell of two-way table.

Let's see if each statement is one that we can justify from the given mosaic plot.

###"Cyclists wearing a helmet were less likely to die in an accident than cyclists without a helmet."

The height of the "Death" segment is shorter in the "Yes" column than in the "No" column.

So we *can* justify this statement from the mosaic plot.

###"Cyclists not wearing a helmet were more likely to have a major injury than a minor injury."

If the cyclist was not wearing a helmet, the relative frequency of getting a minor injury was about $25\%$ and the relative frequency of getting a major injury was about $70\%$.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

If we didn't have relative frequencies labeled, we could still see that the "Major injury" segment was taller than the "Minor injury" segment.

So we *can* justify this statement from the mosaic plot.

###"More than half of all cyclists in the accidents were wearing a helmet."

The "Yes" column is wider than the "No" column. So a larger percentage of the cyclists in the accidents were wearing helmets than not wearing them.

So we *can* justify this statement from the mosaic plot.

###Answers

We can justify these statements from the mosaic plot:

* Cyclists wearing a helmet were less likely to die in an accident than cyclists without a helmet.
* Cyclists not wearing a helmet were more likely to have a major injury than a minor injury.
* More than half of all cyclists in the accidents were wearing a helmet.

choices.content: Cyclists wearing a helmet were less likely to die in an accident than cyclists without a helmet.
Cyclists not wearing a helmet were more likely to have a major injury than a minor injury.
More than half of all cyclists in the accidents were wearing a helmet.

assessment_items.question_content:
Ms. Chen surveyed a random sample of $750$ families from three districts to see whether they had internet access at home. Here are the results:

 | Internet access | No internet access | Total
- | -: | -: | -:
District 1| $180$ | $220$ | $400$
District 2| $143$ | $57$ | $200$
District 3| $98$ | $52$ | $150$
Total| $421$ | $329$ | $750$

Ms. Chen wants to see whether she can predict whether a family has Internet access based on which district they live in.

**Which mosaic plot should Ms. Chen use?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Ms. Chen wants to use the district to predict internet access. To do this, she needs to know if internet access is similar or different from one district to another.

So she needs the plot that shows the conditional distribution of internet access in each of the districts.

##Plot A

Each column of plot A represents one district. Within the column, the heights of each segment are proportional to the percentage of families in that district who belong to each internet access category.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/b2035b171e9172fed2b24212b0fc51357a4b5e49)

Plot A shows the conditional distribution of internet access by district, so Ms. Chen can use plot A.

##Plot B

Each column of plot B represents one internet access category. Within the column, the heights of each segment are proportional to the percentage of families in that internet access category who live in each district.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/ac47cfe46587a09ddda5301f2401ef59a7528b4c)

Plot B shows the conditional distribution of district by internet access, so Ms. Chen *cannot* use plot B.

##Conclusion

Ms. Chen should use plot A.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/b2035b171e9172fed2b24212b0fc51357a4b5e49)

assessment_items.question_content:
The students at Barton Academy are planning a fundraiser to give money to an organization in their community. They voted for which kind of organization they would like to support. Here are the results:

 | Health | Justice | Animal | Total
- | -: | -: | -: | -:
9th| $52$ | $24$ | $59$ | $135$
10th| $49$ | $69$ | $33$ | $151$
11th| $43$ | $28$ | $29$ | $100$
12th| $68$ | $56$ | $68$ | $192$
Total| $212$ | $177$ | $189$ | $578$

The treasurer wants to see if a student's grade level can help them predict the kind of organization the student voted for. 

**Which mosaic plot should the treasurer use?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The treasurer wants to use the grade level to predict the type of organization. To do this, they need to know if the distribution of types of organization is similar or different from one grade level to another.

So they need the plot that shows the conditional distribution of type of organization in each of the grades.

##Plot A

Each column of plot A represents one organization type. Within the column, the heights of each segment are proportional to the percentage of students that voted for that organization type who belong to each grade level.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/b5e58c2684937911ffe84c39eb19a16b87202891)

Plot A shows the conditional distribution of grade level by type of organization, so the treasurer *cannot* use plot A.

##Plot B

Each column of plot B represents one grade level. Within the column, the heights of each segment are proportional to the percentage of students in that grade level who voted for each type of organization.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/2215dfbc14a9b644546b135965797d02ff54e56c)

Plot B shows the conditional distribution of type of organization by grade level, so the treasurer can use plot B.

##Conclusion

The treasurer should use plot B.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/2215dfbc14a9b644546b135965797d02ff54e56c)

assessment_items.question_content:
The following table summarizes the numbers of grocery stores in $3$ neighborhoods and classifies them by the percentage of their inventory that is fresh produce.

|| **Number of grocery stores** ||
 | Tink Place | Lilad Cross | Bron East | Total
- | -: | -: | -: | -:
$<10\%$ of inventory| $4$ | $3$ | $5$ | $12$
$\geq 10\%$ of inventory| $2$ | $7$ | $10$ | $19$
Total| $6$ | $10$ | $15$ | $31$

Tage is curious whether the neighborhood of a grocery store will help him predict how much fresh produce the store has.

**Which mosaic plot should Tage use?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Tage wants to use the neighborhood to predict the percentage of fresh produce. To do this, he needs to know if the distribution of percentage of produce is similar or different from one neighborhood to another.

So he needs the plot that shows the conditional distribution of percentage of produce in each of the neighborhoods.

##Plot A

Each column of plot A represents one produce category. Within the column, the heights of each segment are proportional to the percentage of stores from that category that are in each neighborhood.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/6f7202bd6fcaf0259d810f2764769cbe6cc8d975)

Plot A shows the conditional distribution of neighborhood by percentage of produce, so Tage *cannot* use plot A.

##Plot B

Each column of plot B represents one neighborhood. Within the column, the heights of each segment are proportional to the percentage of grocery stores in that neighborhood that had each percentage of fresh produce.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/015466fec430045102061307210d2cede9c9c206)

Plot B shows the distribution of percentage of fresh produce by neighborhood, so Tage can use plot B.

##Conclusion

Tage should use plot B.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/015466fec430045102061307210d2cede9c9c206)

assessment_items.question_content:
The administrators of ABC University are concerned about the gender gap in their computer science program. They're also curious about whether or not these students work in computer science once they graduate the program.

The following table summarizes the work placements of the most recent $200$ graduates from the computer science program.

|| Are graduates of ABC University's computer science program working in computer science now? ||
 | Male | Not male | Total
- | -: | -: | -:
Yes| $75$ | $22$ | $97$
No| $67$ | $36$ | $103$
Total| $142$ | $58$ | $200$

The administrators want to know whether a student's gender will help them predict whether the student works in computer science after graduation.

**Which mosaic plot should the administrators use?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The administrators want to use the student's gender to predict the work placement (computer science or not computer science). To do this, they need to know if the distribution of work placement is similar or different from one gender to another.

So they need the plot that shows the conditional distribution of work placement for each of the genders.

##Plot A

Each column of plot A represents one gender category. Within the column, the heights of each segment are proportional to the percentage of graduates from that category that are in each work placement.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/fbe40d1359b7171d44dd6829ed3dec085bc799ca)

Plot A shows the conditional distribution of work placement by gender, so the administrators can use plot A.

##Plot B

Each column of plot B represents one job placement category. Within the column, the heights of each segment are proportional to the percentage of graduates in that job placement from each gender.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/62e9588bee4f33a88c9f3c5eef53197bda656fae)

Plot B shows the distribution of gender by job placement, so the administrators *cannot* use plot B.

##Conclusion

The administrators should use plot A.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/fbe40d1359b7171d44dd6829ed3dec085bc799ca)
